topic: Introduction to Pulmonology / Investigations of the Respiratory System

cards.q: What is pulmonology?
cards.a: A field of medicine that focuses specifically on diagnosing and treating disorders of the respiratory system.

cards.q: What are the other names for pulmonology?
cards.a: Respirology, respiratory medicine or chest medicine.

cards.q: From what structure does the respiratory system develop?
cards.a: As an outgrowth from the ventral (front) wall of the foregut in week 4.

cards.q: Into what two channels is the foregut split?
cards.a: One going to the stomach (the oesophagus) and one going to the lungs (the trachea).

cards.q: What is the germ-layer origin of the epithelial lining from the larynx to the alveoli?
cards.a: Endodermal (from the endoderm).

cards.q: What is the germ-layer origin of the cartilage and muscle around the airways?
cards.a: Mesodermal (from the mesoderm).

cards.q: What is the oesophagotracheal septum and when does it form?
cards.a: The wall of tissue that separates the respiratory bud from the oesophagus; it forms in the 4th week.

cards.q: What does failure of the oesophagotracheal septum cause?
cards.a: Tracheo-oesophageal fistula (TOF) — a connection between the airway and the food pipe, a surgical emergency in newborns.

cards.q: Into what two structures does the oesophagotracheal septum divide the foregut?
cards.a: The respiratory diverticulum anteriorly (becomes the lungs) and the oesophagus posteriorly.

cards.q: At what single point do the respiratory and oesophageal tubes remain connected?
cards.a: Through the larynx.

cards.q: From which branchial arches is the larynx formed?
cards.a: The 4th and 6th branchial arches.

cards.q: How many bronchi and lobes does the left lung bud form?
cards.a: 2 main bronchi and 2 lobes.

cards.q: How many bronchi and lobes does the right lung bud form?
cards.a: 3 main bronchi and 3 lobes.

cards.q: Why is the adult right lung slightly larger?
cards.a: The 3-lobe/2-lobe asymmetry is determined at the embryological lung-bud stage.

cards.q: Name the two developmental phases of the lung given in the lecture and their weeks.
cards.a: Pseudoglandular phase (5-17 weeks) and canalicular phase (15-25 weeks).

cards.q: Why is the pseudoglandular phase called 'false gland'?
cards.a: The lung looks gland-like under the microscope and airways are forming, but there is no gas exchange yet.

cards.q: What happens to the bronchiolar lining cells in the canalicular phase?
cards.a: They thin out and flatten into type I alveolar epithelial cells — the gas-exchange cells.

cards.q: Which cells produce surfactant, and when do they begin?
cards.a: Type II alveolar epithelial cells, beginning in the canalicular phase (15-25 weeks).

cards.q: What is surfactant?
cards.a: A phospholipid coat laid over the alveolar membranes that reduces surface tension at the air-blood capillary interface.

cards.q: What does surfactant do?
cards.a: It reduces surface tension so the alveoli do not collapse inward during expiration, keeping them open for the next breath.

cards.q: Why do premature infants develop RDS?
cards.a: They are born before sufficient surfactant is produced, so their alveoli keep collapsing and breathing becomes exhausting work.

cards.q: How do the lungs grow after birth?
cards.a: By increasing the number of respiratory bronchioles and alveoli — multiplication, not enlargement of existing alveoli.

cards.q: What does 'continuum of mucosal surfaces' mean for the respiratory tract?
cards.a: The tract is one connected surface from nose to alveoli, so infection can travel downward along it with no anatomical stop signs.

cards.q: List the respiratory tract in order.
cards.a: Nasal cavity, pharynx, epiglottis, larynx, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, alveolar sacs.

cards.q: At what landmark is the respiratory tract divided into upper and lower?
cards.a: At the larynx — above it is upper, below it is lower.

cards.q: What structures make up the upper respiratory tract?
cards.a: The nose, paranasal sinuses and pharynx.

cards.q: What structures make up the lower respiratory tract?
cards.a: The trachea, bronchi, bronchioles and alveoli.

cards.q: Which serious diseases tend to live in the lower respiratory tract?
cards.a: Pneumonia, asthma and bronchiolitis.

cards.q: Where does the trachea start?
cards.a: At the lower border of the cricoid cartilage at C6.

cards.q: Where does the trachea bifurcate?
cards.a: At the sternal angle of Louis (T4/T5), forming the right and left main bronchi.

cards.q: What are the dimensions of the trachea?
cards.a: About four and a half inches long and nearly one inch in diameter.

cards.q: What is the lining of the trachea?
cards.a: Ciliated columnar epithelium with goblet cells.

cards.q: What is the function of the tracheal cilia and goblet cells?
cards.a: Cilia beat upward to sweep debris out; goblet cells produce mucus to trap pathogens — the first-line defence system.

cards.q: Describe the right main bronchus.
cards.a: Wider, shorter and more vertical than the left, leaving at an angle of 25 degrees to the horizontal.

cards.q: Describe the left main bronchus.
cards.a: Narrower and steeper, leaving at an angle of 45 degrees.

cards.q: Why do foreign bodies preferentially enter the right bronchus?
cards.a: Because it is wider and more vertical — a more direct continuation of the trachea — so aspirated material enters it, particularly the middle and lower lobes of the right lung.

cards.q: If a child inhales something, which side do you examine first?
cards.a: The right — look right first.

cards.q: What is the shape of each lung and where is its apex?
cards.a: Conical, with a blunt apex projecting above the sternal end of the first rib.

cards.q: Why can a penetrating injury at the base of the neck injure the lung?
cards.a: Because the lung apex projects above the first rib, into the root of the neck.

cards.q: How many lobes and fissures does the right lung have?
cards.a: Three lobes (upper, middle, lower) divided by two fissures — the oblique and the horizontal.

cards.q: How many lobes and fissures does the left lung have?
cards.a: Two lobes (upper and lower) divided by one oblique fissure.

cards.q: Why is the left lung smaller?
cards.a: Partly because the heart occupies space on the left side of the chest.

cards.q: Why is downward spread of respiratory infection common?
cards.a: Because the tract is one connected surface, so a pathogen spreads along the tracheobronchial tree from a URTI to bronchitis, bronchiolitis or pneumonia.

cards.q: What is the main function of the respiratory system?
cards.a: To supply sufficient oxygen to meet metabolic demands and remove carbon dioxide.

cards.q: Which three processes are involved in oxygenation and CO2 removal?
cards.a: Ventilation, perfusion and diffusion.

cards.q: What results from abnormality in any one of ventilation, perfusion or diffusion?
cards.a: Respiratory failure.

cards.q: How is respiratory tract disease classified?
cards.a: Into upper respiratory tract infection (URI/URTI) and lower respiratory tract infection (LRI/LRTI).

cards.q: What are the three pillars of evaluation, in order?
cards.a: History taking, physical examination and laboratory investigation.

cards.q: Why does history come first?
cards.a: Because it guides everything else — it tells you what to look for on examination and which investigations to request; without it the workup is unfocused and expensive.

cards.q: What are the five components of the history?
cards.a: Bio-data, presenting symptoms, past medical history, family history and social history.

cards.q: What four things does age give insight into?
cards.a: Anatomical maturity, physiological reserve, likely diagnosis and risk of deterioration.

cards.q: Why do infants obstruct their airways faster?
cards.a: They have smaller airways, and resistance increases dramatically as airway diameter decreases, so small amounts of mucus, oedema or inflammation narrow the airway significantly.

cards.q: Why does the infant chest wall collapse inward during distress?
cards.a: It is highly compliant (floppy) — the ribs are soft and horizontal — causing chest recession and quicker respiratory fatigue.

cards.q: Why do young infants tolerate hypoxia poorly?
cards.a: They have immature respiratory control and poor hypoxic reserve, and can develop apnoea, especially neonates.

cards.q: Why do infants decompensate faster than older children?
cards.a: They have lower physiological reserve — limited oxygen reserve and higher metabolic demand.

cards.q: Are a 2-month-old and an 8-year-old with the same severity of bronchiolitis equally stable?
cards.a: No — the infant has less reserve and is at much higher risk of rapid deterioration.

cards.q: What respiratory diagnoses are common in neonates (<28 days)?
cards.a: Congenital anomalies, neonatal pneumonia and aspiration.

cards.q: What respiratory diagnoses are common in infants (<1 year)?
cards.a: Bronchiolitis and pertussis.

cards.q: What respiratory diagnoses are common in toddlers (1-3 years)?
cards.a: Viral wheeze, foreign body aspiration and croup.

cards.q: What respiratory diagnoses are common in school-age children?
cards.a: Asthma and pneumonia.

cards.q: What respiratory diagnoses are common in adolescents?
cards.a: Asthma and atypical pneumonia.

cards.q: Why does gender matter in respiratory disease?
cards.a: Some conditions have a sex predilection — pneumonia and bronchiolitis are more common in males.

cards.q: What are the three major presenting respiratory symptoms?
cards.a: Cough, breathlessness and chest pain.

cards.q: How should a cough be characterised?
cards.a: By duration, character (barking, whooping, productive), timing (night, morning, after feeding), severity and sputum production.

cards.q: How should breathlessness be characterised?
cards.a: By severity, duration and pattern — constant or episodic, with exercise or at rest.

cards.q: How do you distinguish respiratory from cardiac chest pain?
cards.a: Respiratory (pleuritic) pain relates to breathing and worsens on inspiration or coughing; cardiac pain has a different character and relates to effort.

cards.q: List the six types of noisy breathing you must know.
cards.a: Wheeze, stridor, snuffles, rattles, snoring and grunting.

cards.q: What does stridor indicate versus wheeze?
cards.a: Stridor indicates upper airway obstruction; wheeze indicates lower airway narrowing.

cards.q: Why is grunting significant?
cards.a: It is a danger sign in a distressed infant, who grunts to keep the alveoli open.

cards.q: What do rhinorrhoea and nasal stuffiness point toward?
cards.a: Upper respiratory tract involvement or allergic disease.

cards.q: Why does otitis media accompany URTIs in young children?
cards.a: Because of the short, horizontal Eustachian tube, which lets infection track easily from the throat to the middle ear.

cards.q: What does hoarseness suggest?
cards.a: Laryngeal involvement — croup, laryngitis, or a foreign body at the level of the larynx.

cards.q: What does sore throat point to?
cards.a: Pharyngitis or tonsillitis.

cards.q: Which triad of features is a red flag for TB or lymphoma?
cards.a: Weight loss, neck swelling and night sweats.

cards.q: What five things should the past medical history probe?
cards.a: Whether it has happened before, whether the child was hospitalised, whether it followed a previous infection, the response to previous treatment (bronchodilators/antibiotics), and any seasonal pattern.

cards.q: What pattern in the past history strongly suggests asthma?
cards.a: Recurrent wheezing that responds to bronchodilators and worsens in harmattan.

cards.q: What two things does family history specifically probe?
cards.a: Atopic disorders and chronic cough in the family.

cards.q: What is atopy?
cards.a: The tendency to develop allergic diseases like asthma, eczema and allergic rhinitis, which runs strongly in families.

cards.q: What conditions might a family history of chronic cough point toward?
cards.a: Cystic fibrosis or primary ciliary dyskinesia (genetic bases).

cards.q: What environmental exposures in the social history can trigger respiratory disease?
cards.a: Dust, pets, rugs and fumes.

cards.q: Why is passive smoking a major respiratory risk factor?
cards.a: It damages airway mucosa, impairs ciliary function and increases susceptibility to infection.

cards.q: What can exercise trigger in a child with reactive airway disease?
cards.a: Bronchospasm.

cards.q: Why is overcrowding a respiratory risk factor?
cards.a: It is a major risk factor for the spread of respiratory infections, particularly TB and pneumonia.

cards.q: OSPE — On a diagram of the respiratory tract, name the structures air passes from the nose to the alveoli.
cards.a: Nasal cavity, pharynx, epiglottis, larynx, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, alveolar sacs.

cards.q: OSPE — A diagram shows the two main bronchi. Which is which and how do you tell?
cards.a: The right is wider, shorter and more vertical (25 degrees); the left is narrower and steeper (45 degrees).

cards.q: OSPE — Point to and name the landmark for the tracheal bifurcation on a chest surface diagram.
cards.a: The sternal angle of Louis, where the manubrium meets the body of the sternum (T4/T5).

cards.q: CLINICAL — A newborn chokes, coughs and becomes cyanosed with the first feed. What embryological defect explains this?
cards.a: Tracheo-oesophageal fistula from failure of the oesophagotracheal septum — a surgical emergency.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A toddler suddenly chokes while eating groundnuts, then develops right-sided reduced air entry. What is the diagnosis and why the right side?
cards.a: Inhaled foreign body — it lodges on the right because the right bronchus is wider and more vertical (a direct continuation of the trachea).

cards.q: CLINICAL — A premature baby develops worsening respiratory distress soon after birth. What is the underlying deficiency?
cards.a: Insufficient surfactant, causing alveolar collapse — respiratory distress syndrome (RDS).

cards.q: CLINICAL — A child has recurrent wheeze that improves with a bronchodilator and worsens in harmattan. What is the likely diagnosis?
cards.a: Bronchial (childhood) asthma — reversible airway obstruction with environmental trigger worsening.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A child presents with chronic cough, weight loss, neck swelling and night sweats. What must you exclude?
cards.a: Tuberculosis (and lymphoma) — this red-flag triad must never be missed.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why can a mild upper respiratory infection in an infant progress to pneumonia within days?
cards.a: Because the respiratory tract is one continuous mucosal surface, so the pathogen spreads downward along the tracheobronchial tree.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why is a barking cough with hoarseness significant, and what does it suggest?
cards.a: It indicates laryngeal involvement — croup — reflecting upper-airway/laryngeal narrowing.
